segments.text: Thanks for your patience.
Uh, the earlier I outcome went longer than we
thought. That means
You can cross examinee us for less time.
Quick fire. Quick fire. Are you feeling happy about that?
Is it?
Thank you. Thank you so much.
So now we have, um, yeah, um, uh, until, uh, two o'clock.
And if you would like to start by introducing yourselves
and then some opening remarks.
If you could keep your opening remarks really, really brief,
like around two minutes.
And then we will have questions from members.
Thank you. Thank you.
Um, good afternoon, Kira. I'm Susie Staley.
I'm the chair of the Broadcasting Standards Authority
and of course I have here my Chief executive Stacey Wood.
And, uh, she will complete the opening remarks.
Thank you Madam Chair.
Thank you. Thank you. Sorry.
Under two minutes I'll do my best. Uh,
Under two minutes.
Under two minutes, yeah.
In two minutes max. I know it's tough.
Alright. It's been, uh, two
and a half years since we last appeared in front of you.
So I will just give a really quick, uh, refresher on
who we are and what we do.
Uh, the BSA is a independent crown entity,
a PSI judicial tribunal.
Uh, we're governed by a four person board, chaired by Susie,
uh, supported by eight staff, including me.
So we are not quite the smallest crown
entity, but we are pretty close.
Um, we exist to uphold standards and broadcasting.
Uh, so our jurisdiction is over programs.
It's audio visual content transmitted to the public
by any means of telecommunication.
And traditionally, this has meant terrestrial
and satellite TV and radio.
And our act specifically excludes on demand content,
um, with the huge change to how people consume media.
Since our act was passed in 1989, uh,
it's no longer exactly clear where the edges
of our jurisdiction are.
And we've had some advice that it may include online radio
or TV stations, uh, but not the likes of Netflix, of course,
because of the on demand exclusion
with reg regulatory reform of our act being on
and off the agenda in the last few years.
And with our limited resources,
we've held off on a full review
of our complaints in maybe jurisdiction.
Uh, but we do need legislative clarity, uh,
to bring our act into the present.
And even our most traditional broadcasters are moving
towards digital only offerings.
So they need that clarity as well.
Uh, our vision is freedom of expression without harm, uh,
which is simple to say, but not
always simple to give a feature.
Two. But I'll leave it there. Thank, thank you.
And happy to answer any questions you have. Yeah.
We'll start with Uben Davidson.
Thank you. And thank you for that, um, for that opening.
You, you mentioned in the, in your opening comments, um,
alternative platforms for content.
So we know more New Zealand as a consuming content
through online platforms like social media.
You'd agree with that? Yes. Yeah.
So that content currently sits outside of the BSA
regulatory remit, but do you see the similarities
between some of that content
where perhaps on a platform you can find a 40 minute
video, for example, on YouTube?
Uh, it's the same format as a documentary or radio program.
Are you, you're finding this kind
of thing across multiple platforms?
Can I, uh, just before you answer,
I know you can answer the question.
Can I say that this is the, uh,
review briefing on 20 23, 20 24, um,
annual review of broadcasting.
So if you want to bring your questions to the annual review,
rather than having a general policy discussion
or a senior opinions on that.
So if you bring it back to that,
that would be fine. I'll let the question
Go ahead. So evidenced
in, in the review is that shift
of viewership from, uh, traditional terrestrial
or broadcast media into online platforms.
Um, and on those online platforms, part of that shift is
because the equivalent content now sits on those online
platforms as opposed to in the broadcast
ones that are measured here.
Um, so I, I guess the question here is,
um, that shift, are you tracking
actively where the, the platforms
that are carrying that content?
Yeah, we, we are not tracking it.
Uh, however, we are conscious and we do have research
and surveys, not just by ourselves,
but by others that show
where those audiences are accessing their content.
There is an argument for a platform platform agnostic
approach to regulation where we are looking at who,
who's producing the content rather than
where are they seeing, where are people watching it.
Mm-hmm. Uh, because we know there's, you know,
there's a huge range of approaches to regulation
of social media and it's quite a different concept
to the regulation of, uh, broadcast content.
There's some different concepts of harm prevention
and safety that you're trying to address.
So it is not a one size fits all approach.
Um, I don't know if that answers your question again.
Can I add, can I add that?
I mean, as you'll be aware, we're not a policy shop. Mm-hmm.
We certainly keep an eye on with our resources
and we only have certain amount of resources
and we're conscious of using them correctly.
We give input to, as a, we hope an expert advisor
to the, to the ministry on possible changes.
Um, and it's fair to say that in this area,
every jurisdiction, certainly democratic jurisdictions are
struggling you to see what's happening in the uk uh,
Australia and the eu.
So Stacey did join a group some years ago now, uh,
and to Ireland to have a look at what they're doing.
But nothing has particularly moved on in New Zealand.
It, it is hard, and we are not really there at the moment.
We, we do struggle sometimes with our definition
of broadcasting, which is 35 years old. Mm-hmm.
Yeah. Quite a lot's changed in most days. Yes, it has.
So, um, just, just one or two things.
So a a furthest up on that I guess would be, given
that you have a really, um,
or you have a good understanding of what you are regulating,
that doesn't necessarily mean that viewers do.
So are you receiving complaints from people for content
that in your regulatory framework isn't broadcasting?
Yeah, we, we regularly get inquiries
and complaints from people about content that we can't
or don't regulate or,
and sometimes it's an easy matter of redirecting them
to the media council or advertising standards authority.
And other times it's saying, um,
tell Facebook your problems.
Um, we, and nobody has regulatory purview over that.
Yeah. And are you, are you measuring the, um, complaints
that aren't covered?
We measure them as a blog, um,
but we don't track where everyone's going.
So it's probably around a quarter to a fifth of inquiries
that we get, uh, uh, fluctuates over time.
People got, people get, um, interested in different issues.
Yeah. Uh, but that's, that's about it. You know,
We, we have, it's a very interesting question
'cause I asked my chief executive the same,
but we would say about 20% of our staff's time
or is spent, uh, triaging and sort of arbitrage.
We have a policy of no wrong door
amongst the, uh, authorities.
So it might just be say,
well this is actually advertising standards.
But you're right. There's still a gap, uh,
and sort of, uh, NetSafe of course, uh,
but there isn't any other regulated that's dealing
with the platforms for which you've referred.
We, we also don't have a good idea of
how many people would complain if they knew there was a
complaints process to complain through.
Yeah.
So just to up on that,
because a lot of content that people discover online
originated in a broadcast medium.
So if someone wants to complain about a piece of content
that they've seen on an unnamed platform
and they come to you, but you know that it originate,
do you redirect people to say,
try re try resubmitting this complaint
with the original broadcast details? Uh,
Yeah. If they're able to provide
the original broadcast details
and the original broadcasters within our timeframe,
then we can accept it.
Yeah. Uh, so if it's got a clear source on it,
that's one of our broadcasters.
If it says one news on it, uh, we can advise 'em
to contact the broadcaster
and, um, find out the original broadcast date.
But if it's outside of that timeframe,
then even though it's still accessible online,
can no longer take a complaint about it.
And just to be really clear on that,
'cause you're saying that's something you can do,
but is that something that you proactively do?
Yep. Yep.
Alright. Uh, new question from Linda.
Yeah. It's a new question, but also
Sort of a sub along the same lines as my colleague.
'cause I'm wondering about how,
and it's great that you've said

In this space. Um,
and understanding, you know, that front facing
customer service type approach, um,

'cause we've heard it in other spaces that, um,

ever changing environment.

And so we put out, for example, joint comms

Uh, we can inform watches

All the while knowing that if it's advertising on social

Yeah, yeah. No,
Just Stop on that.

differing views in terms of the, the quality of the handling
of the complaints and, um, you know,
some people are satisfied

but also, and it's, uh,
customer satisfaction is something very different,
but, you know, you're always trying to look for ways
with which to standardize and improve
Practice. Absolutely. So
part of our oversight
of the standard system is,
and our engagement functions is to check in with

So we do the complainant survey in years,

people are a bit more dissatisfied just

Feel free to tell us that.
But, but generally Language that I couldn't possibly
Repeat, generally still happy with the process

Uh, we do litmus testing where we go out

of decisions on a topic or a standard
and say, explain the reasoning and then say, do you agree?
And generally those results are sort of around the 90% mark

they understand why a decision's been made the way it has.
So we have pretty robust evidence externally

consistently and clearly,
and that our staff are treating
people with care and respect.
And we also do research with, in terms of, um,
efficiency and costs.
We are now move that to every two years,

with that is reviewed.
Uh, it's a research piece of paper usually

with complaints. Thank you.

how do you project out that, um, the future work and,

how far in advance you identify other

period, and that's how we do it.
And it's on a rolling basis.
So it's different, different areas

with our guidance and our yeah.
Advisories, uh, where we can see that there's a number

At. Yeah. And a

we all remember COVID, we've got a, a large number

just uh, on that.

what expansion were you predicting in that year to, to now?

and what our role would be, uh, we wouldn't,

That would, uh, that would take a lot

Thank you. Uh,
Thank you. Um,

they've had a little bit of time to bid in,

Um, when people are coming to you

How are you balancing that now

Well, I suppose the answer is we can only deal

It must be about a broadcast.
So there's a threshold
and it must have first of all, gone to the broadcaster.
So I don't know if this is quite answering you.
So what we are seeing, and you mentioned accuracy,
and it's interesting, we debate this ourselves.
There is now a number of complaints
where they are biases used.
Bias is not a standard and media are allowed to be biased.
It's editorial discretion.
Um, and usually it's tied up with really,
it's an accuracy complaints or balance complaints.
So you sort of triage them.
And of course we do have the ability to add, uh,
if we think it's in the interest of the complaint,
that they really should have complained under balance.
Um, I think increasingly the, we do struggle to a degree.
It's interesting. We have bias usually against
all of you politicians.
And it's usually one, we get them evenly balanced.
So, so, so you know, that bias, that's not how it feels.

that is editorial discretion.

with societal norms and we hope that we reflect that.
I don't think we are there to run ahead.
We are to there to reflect societal norms.
Yes. If I can. Thank you.

Um, and, and we had quite a backlog of complaints.

In the last couple of years,

first to average levels.

Uh, we only got 89 formal complaints,

Might point to also what you're saying,
that's what we are saying as well.
Yeah. So it points to a quite a mature

It points to diminishing traditional broadcasting audiences,
but it also points to, um, broadcasters,
broadcasters understanding the
standards and applying them well.
Uh, and also dealing really well

And they treat people generally

still gets a written decision explaining why they've decline

As a sta helps. Yeah.
